food_name: Kopi (Singapore coffee with sweetened condensed milk, hot — standard kopitiam preparation)

local_names.hindi: कोपी (मीठे गाढ़े दूध के साथ सिंगापुर की कॉफ़ी)

aliases: Kopi (Singapore coffee with sweetened condensed milk, hot — standard kopitiam preparation)
कोपी (मीठे गाढ़े दूध के साथ सिंगापुर की कॉफ़ी)

hindi: कोपी (मीठे गाढ़े दूध के साथ सिंगापुर की कॉफ़ी)

region_of_origin: Singapore — evolved from Hainanese and Fujian immigrant coffee culture; Robusta beans roasted Nanyang-style with sugar and butter or margarine
food_subgroup: Hot coffee beverage with sweetened condensed milk; Robusta coffee concentrate brewed through a cloth filter sock

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has coffee beverage as a category. Singapore Kopi (condensed milk + Robusta coffee) has no specific FoodOn entry; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Kopi — Singapore kopitiam Robusta coffee with sweetened condensed milk (hot)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 entry for Singapore Kopi; A0KZJ (coffee beverage) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Singapore Kopi specifically; not fabricated.

main_ingredients: Robusta coffee beans (Nanyang-roasted with sugar and butter/margarine)

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is 1 cup (220 ml / 220 g), as served at a Singapore kopitiam.

nutrient_source.title: Kopi (condensed milk coffee) — Health Promotion Board Singapore, Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kopi (condensed milk coffee). This is an exact match to the standard kopitiam preparation. HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — coffee with sweetened condensed milk; GI estimated at 84 (range 80–90) based on condensed milk sugar load and Robusta coffee matrix
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Singapore Kopi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 84 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) sweetened condensed milk GI ~80–84 (University of Sydney GI Database, condensed milk entries); (2) the dominant sugar contributor in Kopi is sucrose from condensed milk (~8.8g/100g, ~19.4g per 220g serving); (3) Robusta coffee matrix does not materially lower GI. GL per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (84 × 20.0) / 100 = 16.8. This is classified as high GI (≥70) and high GL (≥20 borderline). Estimation basis documented per Project Veritas v2 GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 38 mg/100 g; caffeine 100–150 mg per cup
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is moderate at 38 mg/100 g (~84 mg per 220 g cup), primarily from condensed milk; this is below the threshold for high sodium concern. However, Robusta coffee delivers 100–150 mg caffeine per cup (higher than Arabica), which transiently raises systolic blood pressure; habitual caffeine consumers develop tolerance, but patients with uncontrolled hypertension should limit intake to one cup per day and consider switching to Kopi-O Kosong (black, unsweetened) to eliminate the condensed milk sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 84 (high), 19.4 g sugar per 220 g serving from sweetened condensed milk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kopi is a high-priority dietary counselling target for patients with Type 2 Diabetes in Singapore: a single 220 g cup delivers approximately 19.4 g sugar (GI 84) entirely from sweetened condensed milk, and patients who drink kopi twice daily consume 38–44 g hidden sugar per day from beverages alone — equivalent to 8–9 teaspoons of sugar — without typically reporting this to healthcare providers; patients should be counselled to switch to Kopi-O Kosong (black, no sugar, no milk) or Kopi-C Siu Dai (evaporated milk, less sugar) and to count kopi as a significant carbohydrate source in their daily meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.0 g/100 g (2.2 g per serving); cholesterol 5 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat is low at 1.0 g/100 g (2.2 g per 220 g cup) and cholesterol is 5 mg/100 g (11 mg per cup), both within acceptable limits for cardiovascular health; the primary cardiovascular concern is the cumulative sugar load from daily kopi consumption contributing to dyslipidaemia and metabolic syndrome rather than direct saturated fat or cholesterol burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kopi (咖啡) is Singapore's most fundamental and culturally anchored beverage — far more than a drink, it is a daily ritual, a social institution, and the heartbeat of the kopitiam (coffee shop). Every morning, from before dawn, the kopi uncle (coffee maker) at every kopitiam brews a thick, dark, aromatic coffee extract using the traditional cloth sock filter ('kopi sock'), and the queue forms. The coffee used is a uniquely local blend: Robusta beans (stronger, more bitter, and more caffeinated than Arabica) roasted Nanyang-style with sugar and butter or margarine, producing beans coated in a caramelised, slightly sticky glaze. The extraction is powerful — a dense, syrupy concentrate that would be undrinkable black. Sweetened condensed milk — thick, creamy, and very sweet — is deposited first in the cup, and the hot coffee concentrate is poured over and stirred, dissolving the condensed milk into a sweet, creamy, intensely flavoured cup. The flavour is unique to Singapore: more robust and bitter than Western café coffee, but sweetened by the condensed milk into a distinctive balanced richness. Per 100 g: 52 kcal, 1.2 g protein, 1.5 g fat, 9.2 g carbohydrate, 8.8 g sugar, 38 mg sodium. Per 220 g serving: approximately 114 kcal, 19.4 g sugar, 84 mg sodium. CRITICAL CLINICAL NOTE: A standard Kopi contains approximately 19.4 g sugar per cup — entirely from the condensed milk. Patients who drink kopi twice daily are consuming approximately 38–44 g hidden sugar per day from beverages alone, often without reporting this to healthcare providers. GI is 84 (high), making this a high-priority beverage for diabetes management counselling in Singapore. Patients should be advised to consider Kopi-O Kosong (black, unsweetened) as a lower-sugar alternative.
meal_context: traditional Nanyang breakfast pairing with kaya toast

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2. The entry is an exact match for standard kopitiam Kopi with sweetened condensed milk. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, documented per policy.
notes: Kopi is the traditional coffee of Singapore and Malaysia, a cornerstone of local kopitiam culture. It is distinct from Western-style coffee in both roasting and brewing process. Robusta beans are roasted with sugar, margarine (or butter), and sometimes pineapple skins or maize to create a dark, caramelized, and intensely aromatic bean. The coffee is brewed using a cloth sock (flannel filter) and pulled between two large tin pots to aerate it. The standard Kopi is served with a significant amount of sweetened condensed milk. Monounsaturated fat (-1.0), polyunsaturated fat (-1.0), and water (-1.0) are reported as -1 because these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry for this food; -1 is used per Project Veritas v2 numeric hygiene convention to indicate source does not report this nutrient (not zero, not measured). Hindi local name and aliases added to satisfy Singaporean dish multilingual requirements under G6/Q1/Q8.
